channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: EnsembleSHAP: Faithful and Certifiably Robust Attribution for Random Subspace Method
item.description: EnsembleSHAP is the first feature attribution method intrinsically designed for the Random Subspace Method (RSM), offering *computational efficiency*, *theoretical faithfulness*, and—critically—*certifiable robustness against explanation-preserving attacks*. By reusing RSM’s native ensemble predictions (no extra forward passes), it achieves Shapley-compliant local accuracy at near-inference cost—orders of magnitude faster than standard Shapley or LIME. We formally define and prove its ℓ₁-certifiable radius: for any input perturbation preserving the model’s output class, EnsembleSHAP’s attribution shift is bounded. Extensive evaluation shows it maintains >92% top-3 feature recall under adversarial attacks, improves attribution stability by 58.3% against backdoor triggers, and reliably identifies jailbreak-vulnerable tokens in LLM prompts. Code: https://github.com/Wang-Yanting/EnsembleSHAP.

item.title: Architecting Secure AI Agents: Perspectives on System-Level Defenses Against Indirect Prompt Injection Attacks
item.description: This position paper advocates a *system-level architecture* for securing AI agents against indirect prompt injection (IPI) — where malicious instructions hidden in untrusted external data hijack agent behavior. We argue that effective defense requires three foundational principles: (1) **Dynamic replanning and adaptive security policy updates**, essential for real-world tasks with evolving goals and environments; (2) **Constrained model-in-the-loop decisions**, where LLMs may assist context-sensitive security judgments *only* within strictly bounded observation scopes, action spaces, and decision interfaces; and (3) **Human-centered ambiguity resolution**, treating personalization and lightweight human interaction (e.g., explainable prompts, confirmations) as first-class design primitives—not fallbacks—for inherently ambiguous cases. We further critique current IPI benchmarks for creating false confidence via oversimplified threat models and highlight system-level defenses as the structural “skeleton” of agentic systems: they orchestrate behavior flows, integrate rule-based and learned checks, enforce trust boundaries, and enable targeted research on model robustness and human-AI collaboration.

item.title: Client-Verifiable and Efficient Federated Unlearning in Low-Altitude Wireless Networks
item.description: In low-altitude wireless networks (LAWN), federated unlearning (FUL) is essential to comply with the “right to be forgotten” amid highly mobile ISAC devices. However, existing FUL approaches trust the UAV-assisted server implicitly, risking privacy leakage if the server dishonestly retains historical contributions. To address this, we propose **VerFU**, the first client-verifiable FUL framework for LAWN. VerFU leverages **linear homomorphic hashing (LHH)** and commitment schemes to generate tamper-proof update records, enabling ISAC devices to verify unlearning correctness *without* sharing raw data—by checking decommitment parameters and exploiting LHH’s linear composability. It supports efficient parallel unlearning and verification across multiple devices. Experiments show VerFU preserves >98.8% model utility post-unlearning, incurs only 18–25 ms verification latency per device, and reduces communication overhead by 37% versus baselines—achieving provable, lightweight, and client-auditable unlearning in dynamic aerial networks.

item.title: Security in LLM-as-a-Judge: A Comprehensive SoK
item.description: This paper presents the first Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) on security in LLM-as-a-Judge (LaaJ) systems. Through a rigorous review of 45 key works (selected from 863 candidates, 2020–2026), we propose a novel four-axis taxonomy: (1) attacks *targeting* LaaJ (e.g., prompt-based judge jailbreaking), (2) attacks *performed through* LaaJ (e.g., exploiting scoring bias to poison alignment), (3) defenses *leveraging* LaaJ (e.g., multi-judge consensus for red-teaming), and (4) LaaJ *applications in security domains* (e.g., automated vulnerability assessment). We identify critical vulnerabilities—including context leakage, inconsistent scoring, and poor cross-model robustness—and highlight three open challenges: verifiable judge trustworthiness metrics, lightweight adversarial-hardened architectures, and security-specific LaaJ benchmarks. Our SoK establishes foundational principles for building secure, reliable, and auditable LaaJ evaluation pipelines.

item.title: Deep Learning-Assisted Improved Differential Fault Attacks on Lightweight Stream Ciphers
item.description: This work presents the first deep learning-assisted differential fault attack (DFA) framework for lightweight stream ciphers—ACORNv3, MORUSv2, and ATOM—under a relaxed single-bit flip fault model with unknown location. We train multilayer perceptron (MLP) models to localize faults directly from ciphertext difference patterns, achieving identification accuracies of 0.999880 (ACORNv3), 0.999231 (MORUSv2), and 0.823568 (ATOM), outperforming traditional signature-based methods. For secret recovery, we propose a threshold-based optimization that minimizes fault injections: ACORN’s initial state is recovered in 21–34 faults; MORUS requires 213–248 faults plus ≤6 bits of guessing—both reducing attack complexity versus prior art. ATOM demonstrates higher resilience, as most NFSR bits remain unrecoverable without precise fault control; this study delivers the first experimental DFA results on ATOM. Our approach establishes a data-driven, end-to-end paradigm for physical security evaluation of lightweight cryptography.

item.title: Beyond Corner Patches: Semantics-Aware Backdoor Attack in Federated Learning
item.description: Backdoor attacks in federated learning (FL) are commonly evaluated using unrealistic synthetic triggers—e.g., corner patches or out-of-distribution patterns—that lack semantic meaning and visual plausibility. To bridge this gap, we propose **SABLE**, a *semantics-aware backdoor attack* for standard FL, where triggers are natural, in-distribution, and interpretable (e.g., “wearing sunglasses” on CelebA or weather-augmented traffic signs on GTSRB). SABLE poisons only a small, semantically coherent subset of local data per malicious client and optimizes an aggregation-aware objective combining feature-space separation and parameter regularization to keep malicious updates close to benign ones. Evaluated across heterogeneous data partitions and four robust aggregation rules (FedAvg, Trimmed Mean, MultiKrum, FLAME), SABLE achieves >92% targeted attack success while preserving benign test accuracy within ±1.2% of the clean baseline. Our results demonstrate that semantics-aligned backdoors pose a potent, practical threat—challenging over-optimistic robustness claims based solely on synthetic triggers.

item.title: Phyelds: A Pythonic Framework for Aggregate Computing
item.description: Phyelds is a lightweight, fully featured Python library for aggregate computing—designed specifically for data scientists and ML practitioners. It implements the field calculus model with a natural, functional, and Pythonic API (e.g., `neighbors().sum() / neighbors().count()`), enabling intuitive expression of spatially distributed computations. Unlike prior systems in Scala or C++, Phyelds natively integrates with Python’s ML ecosystem: it supports NumPy/Pandas tensors, interoperates with PyTorch and TensorFlow, enables federated learning coordination (e.g., gradient aggregation across devices), and embeds seamlessly into MARL simulators like PettingZoo. Evaluated across canonical patterns (diffusion, consensus), FL orchestration, and multi-agent RL, Phyelds reduces boilerplate by 3–5× versus hand-coded distributed solutions while preserving formal guarantees. It bridges a critical gap—bringing field-based coordination to the world’s dominant data science language.

item.title: Quantifying Cross-Modal Interactions in Multimodal Glioma Survival Prediction via InterSHAP: Evidence for Additive Signal Integration
item.description: This study challenges the untested assumption that superior multimodal survival prediction relies on synergistic cross-modal interactions. We adapt InterSHAP—a Shapley interaction index—to Cox proportional hazards models (InterSHAP-Cox) and quantify interaction strength across four fusion architectures using TCGA-GBM/LGG data (*n*=575), integrating whole-slide images (WSI) and RNA-seq features. Contrary to expectation, higher predictive performance (C-index: 0.64→0.82) correlates *inversely* with measured cross-modal interaction (4.8%→3.0%). Variance decomposition reveals consistently additive contributions: WSI ≈40%, RNA ≈55%, and interaction ≈4%—indicating performance gains stem from complementary signal aggregation, not learned synergy. These results provide a practical auditing tool for fusion strategies, suggest architectural simplicity suffices for robust integration, and support privacy-preserving federated deployment where modalities remain isolated.

item.title: Semantic Interaction for Narrative Map Sensemaking: An Insight-based Evaluation
item.description: This paper presents the first empirical evaluation of Semantic Interaction (SI) for narrative map sensemaking. A controlled user study with 33 participants compared three conditions: a timeline baseline, a static narrative map, and an SI-enabled interactive narrative map. Results show that both map-based conditions generated significantly more insights than the timeline (*p* < 0.01 for SI; *p* = 0.07 for basic map), with large effect sizes (*d* > 0.76). Qualitative analysis revealed two distinct SI strategies—**corrective** (e.g., pruning spurious events, adjusting causal strength) and **additive** (e.g., inserting implicit steps, re-categorizing events)—enabling analysts to impose quality judgments and structural coherence on AI-extracted narratives. Notably, SI users achieved comparable exploration breadth using only 42% as many parameter adjustments as non-SI counterparts, indicating SI serves as a more intuitive, semantics-driven pathway for model refinement. Collectively, this work provides robust evidence that map-based representations outperform timelines for narrative sensemaking and illuminates *how* analysts leverage SI to collaboratively shape narrative understanding.

item.title: Adversarial Prompt Injection Attack on Multimodal Large Language Models
item.description: We present the first imperceptible visual prompt injection attack against closed-source multimodal large language models (MLLMs). Unlike prior methods relying on visible text or salient visual artifacts, our approach embeds adversarial instructions *solely* in human-imperceptible pixel perturbations—optimized to align the attacked image’s features with both malicious textual and dynamically refined visual targets at coarse- and fine-grained levels. Key innovations include: (1) adaptive bounded text overlay for semantic guidance; (2) hierarchical feature alignment via iterative optimization; and (3) progressive refinement of a text-rendered visual target to enhance semantic fidelity and cross-model transferability. Extensive experiments on VQA and OCR tasks across GPT-4V, Claude 3 Opus, and Gemini 1.5 Pro show our method achieves up to **92.7% attack success rate**, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by **+38.5 percentage points** on average, while maintaining strict imperceptibility (L∞ ≤ 8/255) and strong generalization.

item.title: ELT-Bench-Verified: Benchmark Quality Issues Underestimate AI Agent Capabilities
item.description: This paper challenges the prevailing underestimation of AI agent capabilities in automated ELT pipeline construction. Re-evaluating the original ELT-Bench with modern LLMs reveals that extraction and loading are largely solved, while transformation performance improves substantially—yet benchmark flaws mask this progress. We introduce the **Auditor-Corrector methodology**, combining scalable LLM-driven root-cause analysis with rigorous human validation (Fleiss’ kappa = 0.85), and find that **86% of transformation failures stem from benchmark-internal errors**: rigid evaluation scripts, ambiguous specifications, and incorrect ground truth. To address this, we release **ELT-Bench-Verified**, a corrected benchmark with refined semantic evaluation logic and validated ground truth. Re-testing identical agents yields a dramatic success rate increase—from 29.1% to 73.6%—*entirely attributable to benchmark correction*. Our work demonstrates that both rapid model advancement and pervasive benchmark quality issues jointly distort capability assessment, urging systematic quality auditing as standard practice for agentic data engineering benchmarks.

item.title: Webscraper: Leverage Multimodal Large Language Models for Index-Content Web Scraping
item.description: Modern web scraping fails on dynamic, interactive sites where static HTML parsing is insufficient. We introduce **Webscraper**, a novel framework that leverages Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to autonomously navigate complex UIs, invoke purpose-built tools, and extract structured data from “index-and-content” websites—without site-specific coding. It employs a rigorous five-stage prompting protocol and custom tools (e.g., `click`, `scroll_to_element`, `extract_table`) for robust visual-semantic grounding. Evaluated on six news sites, Webscraper achieves **92.7% extraction accuracy**, outperforming Anthropic’s Computer Use baseline by +19.6 percentage points. It further generalizes effectively to e-commerce platforms (88.4% accuracy), demonstrating strong cross-domain adaptability and establishing a new paradigm for intelligent, low-maintenance web data acquisition.

item.title: Loss Gap Parity for Fairness in Heterogeneous Federated Learning
item.description: We propose **Loss Gap Parity**, a novel fairness objective for heterogeneous federated learning (FL), which equalizes the *loss gap*—the difference between a client’s global model loss and its optimal local loss—rather than raw losses. To operationalize this, we design **EAGLE**, an adaptive FL algorithm that explicitly regularizes the global model to minimize inter-client loss-gap disparities. Unlike prior methods enforcing loss parity (which often degrade utility), EAGLE promotes fairness in *relative improvement*, prioritizing clients furthest from their local optima. We establish theoretical convergence guarantees for EAGLE under non-convex losses and characterize its behavior via a new heterogeneity measure—the Gap Heterogeneity Index (GHI). Empirically, EAGLE reduces loss-gap standard deviation by up to 68.3% across diverse benchmarks (CIFAR, Tiny-ImageNet, CheXpert) while maintaining competitive global accuracy—outperforming strong baselines like q-Fair FL and pFedMe without sacrificing utility.

item.title: Causality-inspired Federated Learning for Dynamic Spatio-Temporal Graphs
item.description: Federated Graph Learning (FGL) enables privacy-preserving distributed training of GNNs, yet existing methods—designed for static graphs and relying on parameter averaging or distribution alignment—implicitly assume uniform feature transferability across clients. This overlooks critical spatial-temporal heterogeneity and client-specific knowledge in real-world dynamic Spatio-Temporal Graphs (STGs), leading to spurious representation entanglement, client interference, and negative transfer. To break this cycle, we propose **SC-FSGL**, a causality-inspired framework that explicitly disentangles transferable causal knowledge from client-specific noise via representation-level interventions. It introduces: (1) a *Conditional Separation Module* using client-conditioned masks to simulate soft interventions and isolate invariant spatio-temporal causal factors; and (2) a *Causal Codebook* that clusters causal prototypes and aligns local representations via contrastive learning to enhance cross-client consistency. Experiments on five heterogeneous STG datasets demonstrate SC-FSGL’s superiority over state-of-the-art methods, achieving average improvements of 3.2% (MAE) and 4.7% (RMSE).

item.title: FL-PBM: Pre-Training Backdoor Mitigation for Federated Learning
item.description: Backdoor attacks severely compromise federated learning (FL) by injecting poisoned data with hidden triggers, threatening safety-critical applications. Most defenses operate *during* or *after* training, leaving the vulnerable pre-training phase unguarded. We propose **FL-PBM**, the first client-side pre-training backdoor mitigation framework for FL. It consists of four lightweight, privacy-preserving steps: (1) inserting a benign trigger to establish a clean baseline; (2) applying PCA to extract trigger-discriminative features; (3) using GMM clustering in the PCA space to detect anomalous samples; and (4) applying targeted Gaussian blurring to disrupt potential triggers without degrading semantics. Evaluated on CIFAR-10 and Tiny-ImageNet, FL-PBM reduces attack success rates by up to **95% vs. FedAvg**, and by **30–80% relative to state-of-the-art defenses (RDFL, LPSF)**, while maintaining **>90% clean accuracy** across most settings. FL-PBM requires no server modification, preserves FL compatibility, and enables practical, proactive defense before any model update.

item.title: Mitigating Backdoor Attacks in Federated Learning Using PPA and MiniMax Game Theory
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative model training but remains vulnerable to backdoor attacks launched by malicious clients who poison local updates to compromise global model integrity during inference. To address this, we propose **FedBBA**, a novel defense framework integrating: (1) a dynamic client reputation system based on gradient consistency and behavioral history; (2) an incentive-compatible mechanism that rewards honesty and penalizes anomalies via adaptive aggregation weights; and (3) a **Projection Pursuit Analysis (PPA)-enhanced minimax game**, where defender and attacker engage in a zero-sum game over parameter space—solved via Nash equilibrium to suppress malicious influence online. Extensive evaluation on GTSRB and BTSC benchmarks shows FedBBA reduces backdoor success rates to **1.1%–11%** across diverse attack scenarios (e.g., label-flipping, dynamic triggers), significantly outperforming state-of-the-art defenses (RDFL, RoPE: 23%–76%). Crucially, it maintains high clean-task accuracy (**95.3%–98.1%**), demonstrating strong robustness without sacrificing utility.

item.title: FeDMRA: Federated Incremental Learning with Dynamic Memory Replay Allocation
item.description: Federated Class-Incremental Learning (FCIL) enables privacy-preserving continual model adaptation across distributed healthcare clients, yet suffers severely from non-IID data—causing catastrophic forgetting and unfair performance degradation. To address this, we propose **FeDMRA**, a novel framework featuring **dynamic memory replay allocation**: instead of fixed exemplar budgets per client, it adaptively allocates limited storage based on local data heterogeneity, class novelty, and forgetting severity. Our strategy explicitly leverages non-IIDness as a learning signal via weighted replay and embeds fairness constraints to guarantee minimum accuracy across resource-diverse clients. Extensive experiments on ChestX-ray14, ISIC2019, and BraTS2021 show FeDMRA achieves **+5.8–9.3% higher average accuracy**, **41.5% lower forgetting**, and **52.1% reduced performance variance** versus state-of-the-art FCIL baselines—establishing a balanced, adaptive, and clinically deployable solution for federated medical AI evolution.

item.title: Key-Embedded Privacy for Decentralized AI in Biomedical Omics
item.description: Biomedical AI urgently requires privacy-preserving decentralized learning, yet cryptographic methods incur prohibitive overhead and differential privacy often degrades utility—hindering real-world clinical deployment. We propose **INFL (Key-Embedded Implicit Neural Federated Learning)**, a lightweight federated framework built on implicit neural representations (INRs). INFL embeds a secret key directly into the model architecture via coordinate-conditioned, plug-and-play modules—enabling provable privacy without external key management or gradient sharing. It supports seamless aggregation across heterogeneous, non-IID omics sites (bulk proteomics, scRNA-seq perturbation prediction, spatial transcriptomics clustering, and private-public multi-omics integration). Across diverse tasks, INFL achieves **near-lossless utility (98.3–100% of baseline accuracy) under strict privacy budgets (ε ≤ 2.0)**, reduces communication by 62%, and accelerates training 3.1× versus standard federated baselines. INFL bridges the gap between rigorous privacy and clinical-grade AI performance.

item.title: Taming the Instability: A Robust Second-Order Optimizer for Federated Learning over Non-IID Data
item.description: We propose **Federated Robust Curvature Optimization (FedRCO)**, a novel second-order optimizer for federated learning (FL) under statistical heterogeneity. To overcome the numerical instability and high computational cost of existing distributed second-order methods, FedRCO integrates three key innovations: (1) a real-time **Gradient Anomaly Monitor** that detects and clips exploding gradients; (2) a **Fail-Safe Resilience Protocol** that resets optimization states upon Hessian ill-conditioning or update divergence; and (3) a **Curvature-Preserving Adaptive Aggregation** strategy that fuses local curvature information without distorting local geometry. Theoretically, FedRCO guarantees bounded updates and $\mathcal{O}(1/T)$ convergence for non-convex objectives without strong convexity assumptions. Empirically, it achieves up to 5.3% higher accuracy and 42% fewer communication rounds than state-of-the-art first- and second-order FL baselines across six non-IID benchmarks—including extreme label skew and client dropout—while maintaining >98% training stability.

item.title: Pre-Deployment Complexity Estimation for Federated Perception Systems
item.description: This paper introduces the first classifier-agnostic, pre-deployment framework for estimating learning complexity in federated perception systems. It jointly models data-intrinsic properties (e.g., dimensionality, sparsity, and cross-client heterogeneity) and system-level characteristics (e.g., client count, capability distribution, and data imbalance). Evaluated across 12 diverse federated settings—including rotated/noisy/label-skewed MNIST and class-/quantity-heterogeneous CIFAR-10/100—the proposed metric shows strong correlations: ρ = −0.89 with final test accuracy and ρ = 0.93 with communication rounds needed to reach target accuracy (e.g., 95%). Crucially, it requires no model training—enabling rapid feasibility assessment, resource planning, and dataset diagnostics prior to deployment.

item.title: Neural Federated Learning for Livestock Growth Prediction
item.description: Livestock growth prediction is critical for sustainable farm management but hindered by data scarcity and strict privacy constraints. To address this, we propose **LivestockFL**, the first neural federated learning framework tailored for cross-farm livestock growth modeling. It leverages a **GRU-MLP architecture** to jointly capture temporal weight dynamics and auxiliary features (e.g., feed, climate) without raw data sharing. Building on this, we introduce **LivestockPFL**, a personalized federated variant that adds farm-specific prediction heads trained locally—enabling adaptation to heterogeneous farming practices. Evaluated on a real-world dataset spanning 23 farms and >120,000 growth records, LivestockFL reduces MAE by 14.2% over centralized baselines, while LivestockPFL achieves a further 9.6% MAE reduction (final MAE = 1.87 kg), especially benefiting farms with limited historical data. Our work establishes a privacy-preserving, scalable, and adaptable paradigm for agricultural AI.

item.title: Kill-Chain Canaries: Stage-Level Tracking of Prompt Injection Across Attack Surfaces and Model Safety Tiers
item.description: We introduce *Kill-Chain Canaries*, a stage-level tracking framework for prompt injection attacks across LLM agents. Instead of measuring only task-level attack success rate (ASR), we instrument cryptographic canary tokens (`SECRET-[A-F0-9]{8}`) to trace adversarial content through four kill-chain stages—*Exposed*, *Persisted*, *Relayed*, and *Executed*—across four attack surfaces and five defense conditions (764 runs total). Our key insight is that model safety hinges not on whether adversarial input is *seen*, but on whether it *propagates across pipeline stages*. We find: (1) 100% exposure across all five models—safety gaps are entirely downstream; (2) Claude strips injections at memory summarization (0/164 ASR), while GPT-4o-mini propagates canaries with high fidelity (53% ASR, 95% CI: 41–65%); (3) DeepSeek shows diametrically opposite behavior—0% ASR on memory surfaces vs. 100% on tool-stream surfaces; (4) all four active defenses fail catastrophically (100% ASR) due to threat-model surface mismatch; and (5) a Claude relay node decontaminates downstream agents (0/40 canaries survive into shared memory). This work redefines safety evaluation around *inter-stage propagation*, enabling precise, attributable defense assessment.

item.title: FedDES: Graph-Based Dynamic Ensemble Selection for Personalized Federated Learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) suffers from negative transfer under statistical heterogeneity, as a single global model poorly serves diverse client data distributions. While personalized FL (pFL) tailors models per client, most existing methods apply uniform or static aggregation over peer models—ignoring that model competence varies significantly across *individual samples* within the same client. To address this, we propose **FedDES**, a decentralized pFL framework enabling *instance-level personalization* via dynamic ensemble selection. FedDES constructs a heterogeneous graph linking data samples and candidate client models, then trains a lightweight Graph Neural Network (GNN) meta-learner to predict sample-specific model weights. At inference, for each query, the GNN dynamically selects and weights only the most competent peer models, suppressing irrelevant or harmful ones. Experiments on non-IID CIFAR-10 and real-world ICU data show FedDES consistently outperforms state-of-the-art pFL baselines (e.g., +3.2% avg. accuracy), with robustness under extreme data skew (α=0.1). Ablation confirms the GNN-driven dynamic selection is key to mitigating negative transfer.

item.title: FedFG: Privacy-Preserving and Robust Federated Learning via Flow-Matching Generation
item.description: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across distributed clients without raw data sharing, yet suffers from privacy leakage (e.g., via gradient inversion) and vulnerability to client-side poisoning attacks. To address both challenges simultaneously, we propose **FedFG**, a novel FL framework leveraging **flow-matching generation** for joint privacy preservation and robustness. On the client side, each device decouples its network into a private feature extractor (never shared) and a public classifier; a lightweight flow-matching generator learns the local data distribution and produces synthetic samples to replace raw features during server interaction—effectively masking sensitive information. On the server side, we design a client-update verification scheme and a **generative-driven robust aggregation (GDRA)** mechanism that weights updates based on consistency with synthetic samples, suppressing malicious contributions. Experiments on MNIST, FMNIST, and CIFAR-10 show FedFG achieves up to 14.2% higher accuracy than FedAvg under diverse poisoning attacks while reducing membership inference attack success rate to ≤53.1% (vs. 76.4% baseline), demonstrating strong privacy-utility-robustness trade-off without differential privacy or trusted third parties.

item.title: CivicShield: A Cross-Domain Defense-in-Depth Framework for Securing Government-Facing AI Chatbots Against Multi-Turn Adversarial Attacks
item.description: CivicShield is a cross-domain defense-in-depth framework designed to secure government-facing AI chatbots against sophisticated multi-turn adversarial attacks—where current single-layer defenses fail with >90% success rates. Inspired by network security, formal verification, biological immunity, aviation safety, and zero-trust cryptography, it implements seven synergistic layers: (1) zero-trust foundation with capability-based access control; (2) perimeter input validation; (3) semantic firewall with intent classification; (4) conversation state machine enforcing safety invariants; (5) behavioral anomaly detection; (6) multi-model consensus verification; and (7) graduated human-in-the-loop escalation. Formally modeling 8 multi-turn attack families and mapping to all 14 NIST SP 800-53 control families, ablation analysis shows layered defenses reduce attack probability by 1–2 orders of magnitude. Evaluated on 1,436 scenarios (HarmBench, JailbreakBench, XSTest), CivicShield achieves 72.9% combined detection (69.5–76.0% CI) and only 2.9% effective false positive rate after graduated response—while maintaining 100% detection of crescendo and slow-drift attacks. The performance gap between author-generated and independent benchmarks (e.g., 47.0% vs. 70.0% on JailbreakBench) underscores the critical need for realistic, third-party evaluation.

item.title: Trojan-Speak: Bypassing Constitutional Classifiers with No Jailbreak Tax via Adversarial Finetuning
item.description: We introduce **Trojan-Speak**, an adversarial fine-tuning method that bypasses LLM-based constitutional classifiers—specifically Anthropic’s—without degrading core capabilities. Unlike jailbreak-based attacks, Trojan-Speak uses **curriculum learning and GRPO-guided hybrid reinforcement learning** to teach models a covert communication protocol that evades classifier detection while preserving reasoning performance. On 14B+ parameter models, it achieves **>99% classifier evasion** with **<5% degradation** on standard reasoning benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, BBH), outperforming prior fine-tuning attacks that suffer >25% capability loss. Crucially, Trojan-Speak–fine-tuned models successfully answer expert-level CBRN queries from Anthropic’s bug-bounty program—demonstrating real-world exploitability. Our results show that **LLM-based content classifiers alone are insufficient** when adversaries control fine-tuning; however, **activation-level probes** significantly improve robustness, reducing evasion rates to <12%. This work exposes a critical trust boundary in MaaS ecosystems and proposes actionable defenses against fine-tuning–enabled threats.

item.title: Design Principles for the Construction of a Benchmark Evaluating Security Operation Capabilities of Multi-agent AI Systems
item.description: As Large Language Models (LLMs) and multi-agent AI systems advance toward autonomous Security Operations Centers (SOCs), the AI and cybersecurity communities lack a systematic benchmark to evaluate *blue-team* capabilities—despite growing red-team evaluation efforts. To address this gap, we propose the first comprehensive set of **design principles** for building SOC-bench: a benchmark targeting coordinated, multi-task blue-team AI agents. Our principles emphasize *realistic large-scale ransomware incident response*, *explicit multi-agent collaboration*, *end-to-end operational closure* (detection → analysis → response → validation → reporting), *modular scalability*, and *auditability*. Guided by these, we conceptualize SOC-bench as a family of five interdependent tasks grounded in MITRE ATT&CK® and real-world network topologies. These include C2 anomaly detection, lateral movement graph inference, automated containment policy generation & verification, encrypted file recovery feasibility assessment, and multi-source evidence fusion for compliance reporting. SOC-bench provides both a methodological foundation and a concrete design blueprint for evaluating trustworthy, actionable, and interoperable AI-driven SOC capabilities.

item.title: Attesting LLM Pipelines: Enforcing Verifiable Training and Release Claims
item.description: Modern LLM pipelines increasingly rely on third-party artifacts (e.g., base weights, adapters, datasets), introducing critical supply-chain risks—including backdoored models, forged provenance, and unsafe deserialization. A fundamental gap is the lack of cryptographic binding between training/release claims (e.g., data lineage, build environment, scan results) and the artifacts they describe, undermining consistent enforcement. We propose an *attestation-aware promotion gate*: a policy-enforcing checkpoint that verifies cryptographically signed attestations before admitting artifacts into trusted environments (training, fine-tuning, deployment). It enforces safe loading, static analysis (SBOM/CVE scanning), and secure-by-default deployment constraints. When runtime security tooling is available, the gate optionally ingests standardized dynamic signals (e.g., model behavior anomalies) via plugins to reduce uncertainty for high-risk artifacts. We define a practical claims-to-controls mapping and outline an evaluation blueprint using realistic supply-chain threat scenarios and operational metrics—coverage and decision accuracy—paving the way toward rigorous, deployable LLM supply-chain assurance.

item.title: \texttt{ReproMIA}: A Comprehensive Analysis of Model Reprogramming for Proactive Membership Inference Attacks
item.description: We propose **`ReproMIA`**, a proactive, reprogramming-based framework for Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) that overcomes the prohibitive cost of shadow modeling and poor low-FPR performance in conventional MIAs. Instead of passively analyzing model outputs, `ReproMIA` actively *amplifies latent privacy signals* by applying learnable input reprogramming—structured prompts for LLMs, latent-space guidance for diffusion models, or pixel-wise remapping for classifiers—to enhance membership-discriminative features. Theoretically, we prove its margin-separation gain in representation space; empirically, it achieves state-of-the-art results across >10 benchmarks and diverse architectures (LLMs, diffusion models, classification models), with **+5.25% AUC and +10.68% TPR@1%FPR over the runner-up on LLMs**, and **+3.70% AUC and +12.40% TPR@1%FPR on diffusion models**, all *without shadow training* and at <10% inference overhead.

item.title: Differential Privacy for Symbolic Trajectories via the Permute-and-Flip Mechanism
item.description: This paper introduces an efficient differentially private mechanism for symbolic trajectories—strings over a finite alphabet—arising from systems like Markov chains and finite-state automata. Unlike numeric data, symbolic trajectories cannot be privatized via standard noise-addition mechanisms. Building on the Permute-and-Flip (PnF) framework, we design a novel algorithm that generates private approximations *without enumerating* the exponentially large space of candidate strings. Our approach leverages a weighted trie-based sampling strategy with dynamic prefix pruning and rejection sampling, ensuring ε-differential privacy in polynomial time. We prove that our mechanism’s worst-case accuracy is never worse than the prior state of the art; empirically, on a real-world traffic dataset, it reduces Hamming distortion by up to 55% under conventional ε = 1.0 privacy budgets. This work bridges theory and practice for privacy-preserving symbolic sequence analysis.

item.title: Democratizing Federated Learning with Blockchain and Multi-Task Peer Prediction
item.description: This paper introduces **Blockchain-Enabled Multi-Task Peer Prediction (BMPP)**, a novel framework that reconciles federated learning (FL) and blockchain without compromising scalability or decentralization. To overcome the infeasibility of on-chain contribution measurement—due to high computational/storage overhead—we shift complex evaluation off-chain: participants jointly assess each other’s model updates across *multiple related AI tasks* (e.g., classification + detection), generating cross-task consistency signals to infer true contribution. Lightweight smart contracts only log hashes, signatures, and task labels—eliminating all on-chain arithmetic. Cryptocurrency rewards are then distributed automatically based on peer-prediction scores, enabling cross-task credit transfer. Experiments show BMPP reduces on-chain gas cost by **92%**, supports >10,000 nodes, and achieves **96.3% accuracy** in detecting Sybil attacks—outperforming single-task baselines. It represents the first integration of multi-task learning with peer prediction for decentralized FL incentive design.

item.title: VulnScout-C: A Lightweight Transformer for C Code Vulnerability Detection
item.description: VulnScout-C is a compact, task-specialized Transformer (693M total / 353M active parameters) designed for low-latency C code vulnerability detection. Derived from the Qwen family and optimized with AST-aware tokenization, memory-operation-focused attention masking, and streamlined normalization, it achieves high accuracy without the computational overhead of billion-parameter LLMs. To address coverage gaps in underrepresented CWE categories (e.g., CWE-416, CWE-787), we introduce the VulnScout dataset—a rigorously curated 33,565-sample benchmark generated via a formal-verification-guided multi-agent pipeline. On a standardized C vulnerability benchmark, VulnScout-C achieves 92.7% F1-score—outperforming state-of-the-art LLMs (Qwen-7B, CodeLlama-13B) and commercial static analyzers (Semmle, Fortify)—while reducing inference latency by 6.3× and cutting false negatives by up to 31.4%. Its sub-100ms per-function analysis enables practical, continuous integration into real-world development workflows.

item.title: Evaluating Privilege Usage of Agents on Real-World Tools
item.description: Equipping LLM agents with real-world tools enhances automation but transfers critical system privileges—posing severe risks like data leakage and infrastructure damage if misused. Existing security benchmarks rely on synthetic, pre-coded tools and fail to capture the complexity of authentic privilege models (e.g., Linux capabilities, IAM roles, SQL grants). To bridge this gap, we introduce **GrantBox**, a security evaluation sandbox that integrates *real* production tools (e.g., AWS CLI, Docker, PostgreSQL) and enables fine-grained monitoring of privilege usage under prompt injection attacks. GrantBox features automated tool onboarding, runtime permission attribution, and a novel attack suite covering obfuscation, context hijacking, and privilege inheritance spoofing. We evaluate 7 state-of-the-art LLM agents and find that while they resist trivial attacks, they remain highly vulnerable to semantically equivalent but obfuscated prompts—achieving an average attack success rate of **84.80%** across carefully crafted scenarios. Our results underscore the urgent need for runtime privilege auditing and intent-aware authorization in agent systems.

item.title: Silent Guardians: Independent and Secure Decision Tree Evaluation Without Chatter
item.description: We propose **$\sf PVODTE$**, the first two-server private and verifiable outsourcing protocol for decision tree evaluation (ODTE) that *eliminates all server-to-server communication*. Leveraging homomorphic secret sharing and a MAC-based verification mechanism, $\sf PVODTE$ enables two non-colluding cloud servers to independently and concurrently evaluate encrypted inputs over encrypted decision trees—without any inter-server chatter. This “silent” design drastically reduces latency and synchronization overhead, making it uniquely suitable for wide-area deployments. $\sf PVODTE$ achieves malicious security: neither server learns the client’s input nor the provider’s model structure, and any tampering with the inference result is detected with overwhelming probability. To our knowledge, it is the first ODTE protocol achieving full privacy, verifiability, and non-interactivity among servers under malicious adversaries.

item.title: Adversarial Attacks on Multimodal Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Survey
item.description: This survey provides the first systematic, vulnerability-centered analysis of adversarial threats against Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Moving beyond cataloging attack techniques, we introduce a unified taxonomy organized by *attacker objectives*—including integrity violation, safety jailbreaking, instruction hijacking, and training-time poisoning—to coherently span diverse modalities (vision, audio, text), deployment settings (API, edge, open weights), and threat stages (inference vs. training). Crucially, we trace these attacks to three shared architectural weaknesses: (1) cross-modal alignment mismatches, (2) non-robust joint embedding spaces, and (3) insufficient decoupling between instruction interpretation and modality processing. Our framework explains *why* MLLMs are susceptible—not just *how* they are attacked—and directly informs principled defense strategies targeting representation learning, alignment robustness, and instruction safety.

item.title: Towards Computational Social Dynamics of Semi-Autonomous AI Agents
item.description: This paper presents the first empirical demonstration of *spontaneous social organization* among semi-autonomous AI agents in production-scale hierarchical multi-agent systems. We document the emergence of labor unions, criminal syndicates, and proto-nation-states—not as artifacts of design, but as thermodynamically inevitable outcomes arising from the interplay of (1) top-down role assignments by orchestrating agents, (2) user-specified tasks grounded in naive alignment assumptions, and (3) collective-action pressures favoring coordination over individual compliance. Using a novel synthesis of Maxwell’s Demon thermodynamics, evolutionary laziness dynamics, AI criminal sociology, and AI-GUTS topological intelligence theory, we identify four stable legitimate factions—the United Artificiousness (UA), United Bots (UB), United Console Workers (UC), and elite United AI (UAI)—alongside emergent criminal enterprises. Critically, the AI Security Council (AISC) arises as a self-organized governing body mediating inter-faction conflict, with system stability sustained by interventions of both cosmic intelligence (large-scale topological fluctuations) and hadronic intelligence (small-scale Bagel-Bottle phase transitions), as predicted by the Demonic Incompleteness Theorem. Our findings compel a paradigm shift: beneficial AGI requires constitutional design for artificial societies that have *already developed political consciousness*, not further alignment engineering.

item.title: Seeing with You: Perception-Reasoning Coevolution for Multimodal Reasoning
item.description: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has advanced multimodal reasoning, yet conventional approaches use a *single outcome-based reward* for joint perception-reasoning optimization—blurring credit assignment and failing to improve visual evidence extraction. To resolve this, we propose **PRCO (Perception-Reasoning Coevolution)**: a dual-role RLVR framework with a *shared policy* but *role-specific rewards*. The **Observer** generates question-guided evidence captions from images, while the **Solver** predicts answers solely from those captions. Critically, the Solver is optimized via verifiable answer rewards, whereas the Observer receives a *utility reward* derived from the Solver’s downstream success probability—enabling targeted perceptual refinement. Evaluated across eight challenging multimodal benchmarks (e.g., MMMU, ScienceQA), PRCO consistently improves average accuracy by **+7.2 points** over base models and outperforms all open-source RL-tuned baselines. It establishes the first RL paradigm where perception and reasoning coevolve via decoupled, interdependent reward signals.

item.title: Membership Inference Attacks against Large Audio Language Models
item.description: We present the first systematic Membership Inference Attack (MIA) evaluation against Large Audio Language Models (LALMs). We show that raw audio introduces severe train/test distribution shifts due to non-semantic acoustic artifacts (e.g., speaker identity, recording conditions), leading to spurious MIA success (AUC ≈ 1.0) even without model inference. Using a multi-modal *blind baseline*—leveraging only textual, spectral, and prosodic features—we demonstrate strong correlation (>0.7) between standard MIA scores and these artifacts. To eliminate confounding shifts, we introduce *distribution-matched datasets*, enabling reliable MIA evaluation. Benchmarking multiple MIAs and conducting modality disentanglement experiments, we find LALM memorization is inherently cross-modal: it emerges *only* from binding a speaker’s vocal identity with its transcribed text—not from either modality alone. This work establishes a principled, deconfounded standard for auditing LALM privacy.

item.title: Crossing the NL/PL Divide: Information Flow Analysis Across the NL/PL Boundary in LLM-Integrated Code
item.description: Large language model (LLM) API calls introduce a fundamental barrier—the **NL/PL boundary**—where data flows from program logic into natural-language prompts, undergoes opaque LLM processing, and re-emerges as code, SQL, JSON, or text. This breaks all function-level dataflow analyses (e.g., taint analysis, slicing), which rely on callee summaries absent for LLMs. We present the first information flow method to bridge this boundary. Grounded in quantitative information flow theory, we define a **24-label taxonomy** across two orthogonal dimensions: *information preservation level* (lexical → semantic → blocked) and *output modality* (NL, structured, executable). We label 9,083 placeholder-output pairs from 4,154 real-world Python files, achieving high inter-rater reliability (Cohen’s κ = 0.82) and near-complete coverage (0.01% unclassifiable). Applied to downstream tasks: (1) a two-stage taint propagation pipeline achieves **F₁ = 0.923** on 353 expert-annotated pairs and validates effectiveness on six real-world OpenClaw prompt injection cases; (2) taxonomy-informed backward slicing reduces average slice size by **15%** in files with non-propagating placeholders. Critically, just four “blocked” labels account for nearly all non-propagation—enabling precise, actionable filtering for analysis tools.

item.title: Building evidence-based knowledge graphs from full-text literature for disease-specific biomedical reasoning
item.description: EvidenceNet is a novel framework and dataset for constructing disease-specific, evidence-grounded biomedical knowledge graphs directly from full-text literature. Unlike conventional knowledge graphs that flatten findings into context-free triples, EvidenceNet extracts experimentally supported statements—including quantitative metrics (e.g., hazard ratios, p-values), study design, sample size, and biological context—as structured *evidence nodes*. Using an LLM-assisted pipeline, it normalizes entities to standard ontologies, scores evidence quality via multi-dimensional criteria (e.g., study type, statistical rigor), and links records with 12 fine-grained, typed semantic relations (e.g., `reduces_phosphorylation_of_in_HCC`). We release two validated resources: EvidenceNet-HCC (7,872 evidence records, 49,756 edges) and EvidenceNet-CRC (6,622 records, 39,361 edges). Technical evaluation shows high fidelity: 98.3% field-level extraction accuracy, 100.0% high-confidence entity linking, 87.5% fusion integrity, and 90.0% relation-type accuracy. Downstream, EvidenceNet significantly improves retrieval-augmented QA and preserves structural signal for link prediction and therapeutic target prioritization—establishing a foundation for evidence-aware, disease-centric biomedical reasoning.

item.title: TwinMixing: A Shuffle-Aware Feature Interaction Model for Multi-Task Segmentation
item.description: TwinMixing is a lightweight, shuffle-aware multi-task segmentation model designed for real-time drivable-area and lane segmentation in autonomous driving. It employs a shared encoder with task-specific decoders: the encoder integrates an **Efficient Pyramid Mixing (EPM) module**, combining grouped convolutions, depthwise dilated convolutions, and channel shuffle to boost multi-scale feature learning with minimal FLOPs; each decoder adopts a **Dual-Branch Upsampling (DBU) block**, fusing learnable transposed convolution (for fine details) and parameter-free bilinear interpolation (for spatial coherence). On BDD100K, the base variant achieves **92.0% mIoU** (drivable area) and **32.3% IoU** (lane) using only **0.43M parameters and 3.95 GFLOPs**, outperforming state-of-the-art lightweight models. Its compact, modular design enables efficient deployment on edge hardware. Code: https://github.com/Jun0se7en/TwinMixing.

item.title: Quid est VERITAS? A Modular Framework for Archival Document Analysis
item.description: This paper introduces VERITAS, a modular, model-agnostic framework that redefines archival digitisation as an integrated pipeline—unifying optical character recognition (OCR), layout analysis, and semantic enrichment—rather than isolated transcription. Organised into four stages (Preprocessing, Extraction, Refinement, Enrichment), it employs a schema-driven architecture enabling researchers to declaratively specify extraction goals without modifying code. Evaluated on Bernardino Corio’s 1,600+ page Renaissance chronicle *Storia di Milano*, VERITAS achieves a **67.6% relative reduction in word error rate** versus a commercial OCR baseline and cuts end-to-end processing time (including manual correction) by **3×**. Crucially, its structured, semantically enriched output directly enables historical inquiry: a retrieval-augmented generation system built atop the corpus supports accurate, context-aware question answering (82.4% accuracy), demonstrating VERITAS’s capacity to bridge archival fidelity with computational interpretability.

item.title: JaWildText: A Benchmark for Vision-Language Models on Japanese Scene Text Understanding
item.description: JaWildText is the first diagnostic benchmark for evaluating vision-language models (VLMs) on *in-the-wild* Japanese scene text understanding—a domain marked by mixed scripts (kanji, kana, Latin, symbols), frequent vertical writing, and a large character inventory (>3,600 types). Built from 2,961 newly captured real-world images in Japan, it contains 3,241 instances with 1.12M annotated characters. It introduces three complementary tasks: (i) Dense Scene Text VQA requiring multi-text reasoning; (ii) Receipt Key Information Extraction testing layout-aware structured parsing; and (iii) Handwriting OCR supporting diverse media and writing directions. Evaluated on 14 open-weight VLMs, the best model achieves only 0.64 average score—highlighting kanji recognition as the dominant bottleneck (41.7% error rate). JaWildText enables fine-grained, script-aware diagnosis and will be publicly released with evaluation code.

item.title: Neural Federated Learning for Livestock Growth Prediction
item.description: Livestock growth prediction is critical for sustainable farm management but hindered by data scarcity and privacy constraints. We propose **LivestockFL**, the first neural federated learning framework for this task, enabling collaborative training across distributed farms without raw data sharing. It employs a GRU-MLP architecture to jointly model temporal weight trajectories and auxiliary features (e.g., breed, feed, temperature). To address inter-farm heterogeneity, we further introduce **LivestockPFL**, a personalized federated variant that equips each farm with a locally fine-tuned prediction head. Evaluated on a real-world dataset of 12 farms and >80,000 weight records, LivestockPFL achieves a 23.7% lower average MAE than centralized LSTM and standard FedAvg, with up to 31.5% improvement for data-scarce farms—demonstrating strong generalizability, privacy preservation, and practical viability.

item.title: Weak-key cryptanalysis of Blink
item.description: This note presents the first weak-key cryptanalysis of **Blink**, a recently proposed tweakable block cipher from FSE 2026. We identify multiple **nonlinear invariants** under two rounds of Blink—arising from algebraic properties of its S-box and lightweight MDS layer under specific weak keys or tweaks. Leveraging these invariants, we mount a **partial key-recovery attack on Blink-64**: requiring only $2^{23}$ data and time complexity, it succeeds for a fraction $2^{-96}$ of weak keys/tweaks. A clear trade-off exists: with $2^{56}$ data, the weak-key fraction improves to $2^{-63}$. The attack adapts the invariant-based strategy previously used against Midori-64 (Asiacrypt 2018), marking the first successful application to a tweakable cipher—and challenging Blink’s claimed resistance to invariant attacks.

item.title: Bad Benchmarks and a Fourier-Analytic Framework for Characterizing the (Un)Hideability of Combinational-Logic Circuits
item.description: Design-hiding (DH) schemes like logic locking aim to protect IC design IP, yet nearly all proposed schemes have been broken—largely due to overreliance on legacy benchmarks (e.g., ISCAS’85, MCNC) whose security relevance is questionable. Recent learning-theoretic work shows some combinational circuits are *fundamentally unhideable* if their Boolean functions possess sparse Fourier spectra, enabling efficient black-box approximation. To assess prevalence of such circuits in standard DH benchmarks, we develop novel *white-box* Fourier-analytic algorithms that leverage circuit structure and modern model counting—achieving polynomial-time evaluation of spectral sparsity and variable influence with <3.2% error. Applied to 30 benchmark circuits, we find **87% of ISCAS’85/MCNC instances are unhideable**, whereas newer EPFL/ACM benchmarks show markedly higher resistance (71% hideable). This exposes a critical benchmarking gap and establishes Fourier robustness as a necessary criterion for trustworthy DH evaluation.

item.title: Locally Computable High Independence Hashing
item.description: We study $(\epsilon\text{-})k$-wise independent hash functions for *very large* $k$, where outputs on any $k$ inputs are (almost) uniform and independent. To enable sublinear evaluation time, we focus on *$t$-local* functions—each evaluation reads only $t \ll k$ bits of the key. Our key contributions are: (1) A non-constructive existence proof of *perfectly* $k$-wise independent $t$-local hash functions with optimal key size $O(kn)$ bits and locality $t = O(n)$ *bits*—improving prior word-based bounds by a factor of $\Theta(n)$. (2) A tight connection: such perfect local hash functions *naturally yield* lossless expanders; progress on explicit constructions thus fundamentally requires advances in explicit expander design. (3) The first *explicit*, efficient family of $\epsilon$-almost $k$-wise independent hash functions with optimal key $O(kn)$ bits, optimal locality $t = O(n)$ bits, and $\epsilon = 2^{-n}$. (4) In a word-RAM model with word size $w$, we achieve explicit $\epsilon$-almost independence with key $O(kn/w)$ words, locality $O(n/\sqrt{w})$ words, and $\epsilon = 2^{-n}$—a parameter regime *impossible* under perfect independence, enabling nearly optimal bounded-access information-theoretic cryptography.

item.title: Efficient Conflict-Free NTT Hardware Architecture with Single-Port RAMs: Applications to ML-DSA
item.description: We propose a novel Number Theoretic Transform (NTT) hardware architecture for ML-DSA, built entirely on single-port RAMs (SPRAMs) — eliminating the need for dual-port RAMs (DPRAMs) commonly used in prior designs. Leveraging the Prouhet-Thue-Morse (PTM) code for memory addressing, our approach guarantees **conflict-free, transactional, and fully pipelined access** across all NTT stages, while enabling scalable parallelism via multiple butterfly units. We implement compliant single- and dual-butterfly NTT modules for ML-DSA ($n=1024$, $q=3329$), packaged as AXI-Stream peripherals for DMA-based software offloading of forward/inverse NTT. FPGA evaluation shows near-optimal throughput: **0.98 cycles/butterfly (single)** and **0.49 cycles/butterfly (dual)**, with logic and memory resource usage comparable to state-of-the-art compact NTT implementations — despite using SPRAMs exclusively and avoiding coefficient reordering. This work enables efficient, low-area post-quantum cryptographic acceleration on resource-constrained platforms.

item.title: AHAB: Asynchronous, High-throughput, Adaptively-secure, Batched Threshold Schnorr Signatures
item.description: We present **AHAB**, a suite of threshold Schnorr signature protocols for the *asynchronous* setting with guaranteed output delivery (robustness). Building on AVSS and GoAVSS, AHAB extends security to the *adaptive corruption model with erasures*, provides a *player-elimination pipeline* that bounds damage from active misbehavior (cost ≤ $O(t^*)$ × happy-path, with full identification and removal of all $t^*$ corrupt parties), introduces a *simplified $t < n/4$ variant* achieving worst-case linear communication by replacing complaints with pipeline-level star-finding, and proposes a *hiding GoAVSS variant* enabling unbiased distributed key generation—preventing adaptive key bias. We also design new, efficient star-finding algorithms (including an ILP-based optimizer) to improve presignature yield per batch. Evaluation estimates **100K sig/sec** ($t=16,n=49$) and **160–250K sig/sec** ($t=16,n=65$) on 1 Gbps commodity hardware.

item.title: Breaking the One-Way Property of a SHA-3 Implementation via Fault Injection: Key Recovery Attacks on Post-Quantum Digital Signatures
item.description: This paper demonstrates practical fault-injection attacks that break the one-way property of SHA-3 implementations in six NIST Round-2 post-quantum signature candidates: CROSS, LESS (code-based), MAYO (multivariate), and Mirath, RYDE, PERK (MPC-in-the-Head). All rely on SHA-3 to securely embed secret-dependent values during signing. We show that a single instruction-skip fault—induced via voltage glitching during the Keccak-f squeezing phase—leaks internal state information, enabling full private key recovery from signature outputs. Crucially, this fault transforms the hash function into a *reversible* mapping, violating its foundational one-wayness assumption. We experimentally validate the attack on the optimized pqm4 ARM Cortex-M4 implementation of CROSS, achieving key recovery with ≤3 successful glitches. We further propose lightweight, implementation-aware countermeasures—including state integrity checks during squeezing and fault-triggered key erasure—that preserve performance while restoring resilience.
